  • Hide All Code

Creativity-AI

Claudia Alvarez-Martin, Dan Dediu

2026-02-17

Loading and preparing the data

The data for this study comes from different sources. First, we have the ratings provided by humam participants, which contain also their demographic information. To this information I will add more detailed data regarding their AReA score. Second, we have the ratings obtained from GPT-4O, both with and without human guidance. Finally, we also performed a “Turing test” where new participants were asked to identify the origin (human or GenAI) of the stimuli used in the rating task.

0- Response to the reviewer

Comment 5

Original comment: It would strengthen the study to include multiple human raters evaluating the same images, enabling assessment of inter-rater reliability and reproducibility.

We will provide some measures to assess Inter-rater reliability and reproducibility.

The main problem is that most of the participants in the sample received completely different image datasets to rate, making it difficult to calculate inter-rater measures.

Here we can see the number of images in common for each pair of participants:

We see that the distribution shows some instances where participants rated the same 120 images. We plot again, removing those participants:

We can calculate, for the whole sample, Krippendorff’s alpha:

Number of images Number of raters Alpha
liking 1000 255 0.147498561672397
vividness 1000 255 0.192786490502244
originality 1000 255 0.12938274130992
aesthetics 1000 255 0.105290905929107
curiosity 1000 255 0.107711858658729

Since we have so missing values in the matrix, however, the results obtained are unreliable. We can also computate, for each pair of participants, their Cohen’s d value. However, this measure will also be quite unreliable since so many pairs have very few images in common.

A more reliable way of assessing inter-rater reliability in a case like ours, with unequal raters per item and missingness, is using a mixed-effects model to perform variance decomposition. This allows us to estimate the percentage of variance in the ratings attributed to the effect of the raters and the images separately.

Mixed effects model

## Liking:  0.1450077 
## Vividness:  0.1826286 
## Originality:  0.1284342 
## Aesthetics:  0.1062472 
## Curiosity:  0.1090813 NULL

Reliability is very low (between 10 and 18), meaning that the proportion of total rating variance that is attributable to real differences between images is low.

We can compute another measure of reliability using a split half technique, that will allow us to estimate how stable the image-level scores are. We will randomly split the ratings of each image into two halves and correlate the mean ratings of both subsets, repeating this operation in order to obtain a distribution and calculate a confidence interval.

##      [,1]          [,2]                    [,3]                   
## [1,] "Liking"      "0.706 (0.683 - 0.729)" "0.828 (0.812 - 0.843)"
## [2,] "Vividness"   "0.755 (0.736 - 0.774)" "0.86 (0.848 - 0.873)" 
## [3,] "Originality" "0.667 (0.642 - 0.691)" "0.8 (0.782 - 0.817)"  
## [4,] "Aesthetics"  "0.622 (0.595 - 0.65)"  "0.767 (0.746 - 0.788)"
## [5,] "Curiosity"   "0.625 (0.597 - 0.652)" "0.769 (0.748 - 0.789)"

In this case, the Spearman-Brown corrected reliability of all 5 subscales oscillates between 0.77 and 0.86, indicating good to strong reliability.

Differences in ratings between rater types

## Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
## lmerModLmerTest]
## Formula: creativity ~ raterType * category + (1 | imageId)
##    Data: bd
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 228322.9
## 
## Scaled residuals:
